name: Binary search time complexity
question_text: What is the worst-case time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n elements?
general_feedback: Binary search halves the search space on every step, so after k steps you have n/2k elements remaining. The algorithm stops when that reaches 1, i.e. when k = log2 n, giving O(log n).
correct_feedback: Correct — each step halves the range, giving log₂n steps.
incorrect_feedback: Incorrect — think about how many elements remain after each comparison.

answers.feedback: Right — the search range halves every step.
That would be linear search. Binary search exploits the sorted order to do much better.
That's the cost of a typical comparison sort, not a single search.
Constant time would mean one comparison regardless of n — only true if the target happens to be the middle element.
